The async surface mirrors the sync surface one-for-one. Because diffusers loads model weights in-process, the async factory wraps an existing sync client rather than re-loading weights; passing a HuggingFaceImageModel enum directly is refused with a pointer to the correct pattern.
Async generate() routes the inference call through asyncio.to_thread so the event loop stays free.

title: Image Generation
section: 8. Generate then describe: closing the loop
text:
8. Generate then describe: closing the loop
Pair generate_image with describe_image and the agent can look at what it just produced, then tell the user what’s in it. The factory make_describe_image_tool(client) binds to whatever vision-capable chat client you’re already using (no second model load, no extra API key), and the tool snapshots conversation state so the vision Q&A doesn’t pollute the agent’s history.
Note: on a single GPU, the two gather-ed calls don’t truly overlap on the device (CUDA streams + GIL serialise inference), but the event loop stays free for other coroutines (request handlers, I/O, etc.). For true overlap across GPUs, build separate sync clients per device and wrap each.

title: Image Generation
text:
6. Streaming progress
Pass stream=True to get an iterator of IMAGE_GENERATING chunks during denoising. Each chunk carries step, total_steps, an optional intermediate image (when preview_every=N opts in), and a final flag.
Because the built-in generate_image tool is itself a generator-decorated @tool, these chunks also flow through Agent.run(stream=True) when an agent invokes the tool, with no side-channel callbacks needed.

# Direct streaming, bypassing the agent for clarity.
from aimu import image_client

client = image_client("hf:runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5")

for chunk in client.generate(
    "a watercolor of a fox in a snowy forest",
    stream=True,
    num_inference_steps=20,
    preview_every=5,  # decode VAE every 5 steps for a preview
):
    c = chunk.content
    if c["final"]:
        print(f"\nFinal step {c['step']}/{c['total_steps']}")
        display(c["image"])
    else:
        print(f"step {c['step']}/{c['total_steps']}", end="\r")
        if c["image"] is not None:
            print(f"\n  preview at step {c['step']}:")
            display(c["image"])


# Through an agent: generate_image's progress chunks flow through agent.run(stream=True).
import aimu
from aimu.agents import Agent
from aimu.tools import builtin
from aimu.tools.builtin import make_image_tool

text_client = aimu.client("anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6")
# Use make_image_tool for opt-in previews (the default builtin.generate_image keeps preview_every=None).
img_tool = make_image_tool(image_client("hf:runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5"), preview_every=5)
agent = Agent(text_client, tools=[img_tool])

for chunk in agent.run("draw me a watercolor of a fox in a snowy forest", stream=True):
    if chunk.is_image_progress():
        c = chunk.content
        marker = "✓" if c["final"] else " "
        print(f"  [{marker}] step {c['step']}/{c['total_steps']}")
    elif chunk.is_tool_call():
        print(f"  Tool done: {chunk.content['response']}")
    elif chunk.is_text():
        print(chunk.content, end="")

title: Image Generation
section: 7. Generate then describe: closing the loop
text:
7. Generate then describe: closing the loop
Pair generate_image with describe_image and the agent can look at what it just produced, then tell the user what’s in it. The factory make_describe_image_tool(client) binds to whatever vision-capable chat client you’re already using (no second model load, no extra API key), and the tool snapshots conversation state so the vision Q&A doesn’t pollute the agent’s history.

import aimu
from aimu.agents import Agent
from aimu.tools import builtin
from aimu.tools.builtin import make_describe_image_tool

# A vision-capable chat model (Anthropic Sonnet supports vision and tool calling).
chat_client = aimu.client("anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6")

agent = Agent(
    chat_client,
    system_message=(
        "You can both generate images and describe them. When the user asks for an image, "
        "call generate_image, then call describe_image on the saved path to confirm what "
        "you produced before answering."
    ),
    tools=[builtin.generate_image, make_describe_image_tool(chat_client)],
)

print(agent.run("Make me an image of a fox in a snowy forest, then tell me what's in it."))

title: Evaluations
text:
DeepEvalModel is a thin adapter that wraps any AIMU ModelClient so it can act as the judge model for DeepEval metrics. This lets you use a local Ollama model, a HuggingFace model, or any cloud provider as your evaluator without leaving the AIMU interface.
Install the optional dependency before running: pip install -e '.[deepeval]'

title: Evaluations
text:
A - Setup
Create a ModelClient and wrap it with DeepEvalModel. Any AIMU client can be the judge: swap in a cloud model for more reliable evaluations on complex tasks.

from aimu.evals import HAS_DEEPEVAL, DeepEvalModel
from aimu.models import OllamaClient, OllamaModel

# Use any BaseModelClient as the judge.
# A stronger model produces more consistent judgments.
# from aimu.models import AnthropicClient, AnthropicModel
# judge_client = AnthropicClient(AnthropicModel.CLAUDE_SONNET_4)

print(f"DeepEval available: {HAS_DEEPEVAL}")

judge_client = OllamaClient(OllamaModel.QWEN_3_5_9B)
judge = DeepEvalModel(judge_client)

print(f"Judge model: {judge.get_model_name()}")

title: Evaluations
text:
B - GEval (Custom Criteria)
GEval is DeepEval’s most flexible metric. You describe your evaluation criteria in plain language and the judge model assigns a score (0–1) with a written reason. Use it for any quality dimension: correctness, conciseness, tone, safety, etc.
evaluation_params tells GEval which fields from the test case to pass to the judge. Common choices are INPUT and ACTUAL_OUTPUT; add EXPECTED_OUTPUT when you have a reference answer.

from deepeval.metrics import GEval
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase, LLMTestCaseParams

correctness = GEval(
    name="Correctness",
    criteria=(
        "Determine whether the actual output is factually correct and directly answers "
        "the input question. Penalise hallucinated or inaccurate facts."
    ),
    evaluation_params=[LLMTestCaseParams.INPUT, LLMTestCaseParams.ACTUAL_OUTPUT],
    model=judge,
    threshold=0.5,
)


test_cases = [
    LLMTestCase(
        input="What is the capital of France?",
        actual_output="The capital of France is Paris.",
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="What is the capital of Australia?",
        actual_output="The capital of Australia is Sydney.",  # wrong, it's Canberra
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="Who wrote Romeo and Juliet?",
        actual_output="Romeo and Juliet was written by William Shakespeare.",
    ),
]

for tc in test_cases:
    correctness.measure(tc)
    status = "PASS" if correctness.passed else "FAIL"
    print(f"[{status}] score={correctness.score:.2f}")
    print(f"  Q: {tc.input}")
    print(f"  A: {tc.actual_output}")
    print(f"  Reason: {correctness.reason}")
    print()

Add expected_output and LLMTestCaseParams.EXPECTED_OUTPUT when you have a reference answer. The judge can then compare against the gold standard directly.

correctness_with_ref = GEval(
    name="Correctness (with reference)",
    criteria="Does the actual output convey the same facts as the expected output?",
    evaluation_params=[
        LLMTestCaseParams.INPUT,
        LLMTestCaseParams.ACTUAL_OUTPUT,
        LLMTestCaseParams.EXPECTED_OUTPUT,
    ],
    model=judge,
    threshold=0.5,
)

tc = LLMTestCase(
    input="Summarise the water cycle in one sentence.",
    actual_output="Water evaporates from surfaces, condenses into clouds, and falls back as precipitation.",
    expected_output="The water cycle involves evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.",
)

correctness_with_ref.measure(tc)
print(f"Score: {correctness_with_ref.score:.2f}  Passed: {correctness_with_ref.passed}")
print(f"Reason: {correctness_with_ref.reason}")

title: Evaluations
text:
C - Answer Relevancy
AnswerRelevancyMetric checks whether the response actually addresses the question, useful for catching responses that are accurate but off-topic. It works by extracting claims from the output and classifying each one as relevant or irrelevant to the input.

from deepeval.metrics import AnswerRelevancyMetric

relevancy = AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.5, model=judge)

relevancy_cases = [
    LLMTestCase(
        input="How do I reverse a list in Python?",
        actual_output=(
            "You can reverse a list in Python using list.reverse() to reverse in place, "
            "or list[::-1] to return a new reversed list."
        ),
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="How do I reverse a list in Python?",
        actual_output=(
            "Python is a popular high-level programming language created by Guido van Rossum "
            "and first released in 1991."
        ),  # true, but irrelevant to the question
    ),
]

for tc in relevancy_cases:
    relevancy.measure(tc)
    status = "PASS" if relevancy.passed else "FAIL"
    print(f"[{status}] score={relevancy.score:.2f}")
    print(f"  A: {tc.actual_output[:100]}")
    print(f"  Reason: {relevancy.reason}")
    print()

title: Evaluations
text:
D - Faithfulness (RAG Grounding)
FaithfulnessMetric checks whether every claim in the response is supported by the retrieved context: the core hallucination-detection metric for RAG pipelines. Test cases require a retrieval_context field (list of retrieved chunks).

from deepeval.metrics import FaithfulnessMetric

faithfulness = FaithfulnessMetric(threshold=0.7, model=judge)

context = [
    "The Eiffel Tower is a wrought-iron lattice tower on the Champ de Mars in Paris, France. "
    "It was designed by engineer Gustave Eiffel and completed in 1889 as the entrance arch "
    "for the 1889 World's Fair. It stands 330 metres tall."
]

rag_cases = [
    LLMTestCase(
        input="When was the Eiffel Tower completed and how tall is it?",
        actual_output="The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889 and stands 330 metres tall.",
        retrieval_context=context,
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="When was the Eiffel Tower completed and how tall is it?",
        actual_output=(
            "The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1901 and is the tallest structure in Europe."
        ),  # hallucinated date and unsupported claim
        retrieval_context=context,
    ),
]

for tc in rag_cases:
    faithfulness.measure(tc)
    status = "PASS" if faithfulness.passed else "FAIL"
    print(f"[{status}] score={faithfulness.score:.2f}")
    print(f"  A: {tc.actual_output}")
    print(f"  Reason: {faithfulness.reason}")
    print()

title: Evaluations
text:
E - Batch Evaluation
Run multiple metrics across a list of test cases and collect results into a summary. This is the typical pattern for evaluating a model or pipeline against a labelled dataset before shipping.

batch_cases = [
    LLMTestCase(
        input="What is the boiling point of water at sea level?",
        actual_output="Water boils at 100°C (212°F) at standard atmospheric pressure.",
        retrieval_context=[
            "Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit) at standard "
            "atmospheric pressure (sea level, 1 atm)."
        ],
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="Who invented the telephone?",
        actual_output="The telephone was invented by Alexander Graham Bell, who received the first patent for it in 1876.",
        retrieval_context=[
            "Alexander Graham Bell is widely credited with inventing the telephone and "
            "received the first patent in 1876."
        ],
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="What is the speed of light in a vacuum?",
        actual_output="The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 300,000 kilometres per second.",
        retrieval_context=[
            "The speed of light in a vacuum is exactly 299,792,458 metres per second, "
            "commonly approximated as 300,000 km/s."
        ],
    ),
    LLMTestCase(
        input="What is the chemical formula for table salt?",
        actual_output="Table salt is made of hydrogen and oxygen.",  # wrong
        retrieval_context=[
            "Table salt is sodium chloride, with the chemical formula NaCl. It consists of sodium and chlorine ions."
        ],
    ),
]

batch_correctness = GEval(
    name="Correctness",
    criteria="Is the actual output factually correct and directly responsive to the question?",
    evaluation_params=[LLMTestCaseParams.INPUT, LLMTestCaseParams.ACTUAL_OUTPUT],
    model=judge,
    threshold=0.5,
)
batch_faithfulness = FaithfulnessMetric(threshold=0.7, model=judge)

metrics = [("Correctness", batch_correctness), ("Faithfulness", batch_faithfulness)]


import pandas as pd

rows = []
for tc in batch_cases:
    row = {
        "question": tc.input,
        "answer": tc.actual_output[:60] + "..." if len(tc.actual_output) > 60 else tc.actual_output,
    }
    for name, metric in metrics:
        metric.measure(tc)
        row[f"{name} score"] = round(metric.score, 2)
        row[f"{name} pass"] = metric.passed
    rows.append(row)

results_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print(results_df.to_string(index=False))

print()
for name, _ in metrics:
    pass_rate = results_df[f"{name} pass"].mean()
    mean_score = results_df[f"{name} score"].mean()
    print(f"{name}: mean_score={mean_score:.2f}  pass_rate={pass_rate:.0%}")

title: Prompt Management
text:
PromptCatalog is a SQLite-backed store for the prompts you ship to production. Every Prompt row carries a name, the prompt text, the model_id it targets, an auto-assigned version number, optional mutation/reasoning prompts, and a free-form metrics dict for evaluation scores. Lookups are keyed on (name, model_id), so the same prompt can have independent version lineages per model (e.g. an "assistant" prompt evolving differently on GPT-4 and Claude). Prompts can reference a parent_id to track how one version was derived from another.
Use the catalog when prompts are a moving target: when you’re iterating with PromptTuner (notebook 15), comparing models with Benchmark (notebook 17), or simply wanting a durable record of which prompt produced which results. For ad-hoc one-off prompts you don’t need this; just inline the string.

title: Prompt Management
text:
What this notebook covers



Section
Topic




A
Constructing a Prompt row with metrics and metadata


B
Initialising a SQLite-backed PromptCatalog


C
Storing multiple prompts across versions and models


D
Retrieving the latest version, or every version, of a prompt


E
Listing the model IDs present in the catalog


F
Linking versions with parent_id to track prompt lineage


G
Bulk-deleting all prompts for a model and cleaning up

title: Prompt Management
text:
A - Creating and Managing Prompts
The Prompt class is a SQLAlchemy model that stores prompt data including versioning, metrics, and mutation/reasoning prompts.

from aimu.prompts import Prompt, PromptCatalog

# Create a new prompt instance
prompt = Prompt(
    name="assistant",
    prompt="You are a helpful assistant that provides clear and concise answers.",
    model_id="gpt-4",
    version=1,
    mutation_prompt="Vary the tone to be more friendly",
    reasoning_prompt="Think step by step before answering",
    metrics={"accuracy": 0.95, "response_time": 1.2},
)

title: Prompt Management
text:
B - Setting up a PromptCatalog
The PromptCatalog class provides a SQLite-backed database for storing and retrieving prompts. It handles database connections and provides methods for CRUD operations.

import tempfile
import os

# Create a temporary database file for this example
temp_db = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".db", delete=False)
temp_db.close()

# Initialize the catalog with the database path
catalog = PromptCatalog(temp_db.name)

title: Prompt Management
text:
C - Storing Prompts
You can store prompts in the catalog for later retrieval. Each prompt can be associated with a model ID and version for organization.

# Store the first prompt
catalog.store_prompt(prompt)

# Create and store additional prompts for the same model
prompt_v2 = Prompt(
    name="assistant",
    prompt="You are a helpful assistant that provides detailed explanations with examples.",
    model_id="gpt-4",
    version=2,
    mutation_prompt="Add more technical details",
    reasoning_prompt="Provide reasoning with examples",
    metrics={"accuracy": 0.97, "response_time": 1.5},
)

catalog.store_prompt(prompt_v2)

# Create prompts for a different model
claude_prompt = Prompt(
    name="assistant",
    prompt="You are Claude, an AI assistant created by Anthropic.",
    model_id="claude-3",
    version=1,
    mutation_prompt="Be more conversational",
    reasoning_prompt="Consider multiple perspectives",
    metrics={"accuracy": 0.93, "response_time": 0.8},
)

catalog.store_prompt(claude_prompt)

title: Prompt Management
text:
D - Retrieving Prompts
The catalog provides methods to retrieve the latest prompt for a model, or all prompts for a model ordered by version.

# Retrieve the latest prompt for GPT-4
latest_gpt4 = catalog.retrieve_last("assistant", "gpt-4")
print(f"Latest GPT-4 prompt (v{latest_gpt4.version}): {latest_gpt4.prompt}")
print(f"Metrics: {latest_gpt4.metrics}")
print()

# Retrieve all prompts for GPT-4
all_gpt4 = catalog.retrieve_all("assistant", "gpt-4")
print(f"All GPT-4 prompts ({len(all_gpt4)} total):")
for p in all_gpt4:
    print(f"  v{p.version}: {p.prompt[:50]}...")
print()

# Retrieve the latest prompt for Claude
latest_claude = catalog.retrieve_last("assistant", "claude-3")
print(f"Latest Claude prompt: {latest_claude.prompt}")

title: Prompt Management
text:
E - Managing Model IDs
You can retrieve a list of all model IDs that have prompts stored in the catalog.

# Get all model IDs in the catalog
model_ids = catalog.retrieve_model_ids()
print(f"Models in catalog: {model_ids}")

# Show summary of prompts for each model
for model_id in model_ids:
    prompts = catalog.retrieve_all("assistant", model_id)
    print(f"  {model_id}: {len(prompts)} prompt(s)")

title: Prompt Management
text:
F - Working with Prompt Hierarchies
Prompts can have parent-child relationships through the parent_id field, allowing you to track prompt evolution and mutations.

# Create a child prompt that references the parent
parent_prompt = catalog.retrieve_last("assistant", "gpt-4")

child_prompt = Prompt(
    name="assistant",
    parent_id=parent_prompt.id,
    prompt="You are a helpful assistant that provides detailed explanations with examples and references.",
    model_id="gpt-4",
    version=3,
    mutation_prompt="Add citations and references",
    reasoning_prompt="Provide reasoning with examples and sources",
    metrics={"accuracy": 0.98, "response_time": 1.8},
)

catalog.store_prompt(child_prompt)
print(f"Created child prompt with parent ID: {child_prompt.parent_id}")

# Verify the relationship
updated_latest = catalog.retrieve_last("assistant", "gpt-4")
print(f"Latest prompt version: {updated_latest.version}")
print(f"Parent ID: {updated_latest.parent_id}")

title: Prompt Management
text:
G - Cleaning Up
You can delete all prompts for a specific model when needed.

# Show current state
print("Before cleanup:")
for model_id in catalog.retrieve_model_ids():
    count = len(catalog.retrieve_all("assistant", model_id))
    print(f"  {model_id}: {count} prompt(s)")
print()

# Delete all GPT-4 prompts
deleted_count = catalog.delete_all("assistant", "gpt-4")
print(f"Deleted {deleted_count} GPT-4 prompts")
print()

# Show state after cleanup
print("After cleanup:")
for model_id in catalog.retrieve_model_ids():
    count = len(catalog.retrieve_all("assistant", model_id))
    print(f"  {model_id}: {count} prompt(s)")

# Clean up the temporary database file
del catalog  # This will close the session
os.unlink(temp_db.name)
print(f"Cleaned up temporary database: {temp_db.name}")

title: Memory
text: Demonstrates AIMU’s two complementary memory stores:

title: Memory
text:
A - SemanticMemoryStore: Basic Setup
SemanticMemoryStore stores facts as natural-language strings in subject-predicate-object form (e.g. "Paul works at Google"). By default it uses an in-memory ephemeral store; pass persist_path to save facts to disk across sessions.

from aimu.memory import SemanticMemoryStore

# In-memory store (ephemeral; facts are lost when the process exits)
store = SemanticMemoryStore()

print(f"Store created. Facts stored: {len(store)}")

title: Memory
text:
B - Storing Facts
Use store() to add a fact string. The string should follow subject-predicate-object form, but any natural-language sentence works; ChromaDB embeds it for semantic retrieval.

# Store facts about people
store.store("Paul works at Google")
store.store("Paul is married to Sarah")
store.store("Paul enjoys hiking on weekends")
store.store("Sarah is the sister of Emma")
store.store("Emma lives in Paris")
store.store("Emma teaches mathematics at a university")
store.store("Sarah volunteers at a local animal shelter")

print(f"Facts stored: {len(store)}")

title: Memory
text:
C - Retrieving Facts by Topic
search(topic) uses ChromaDB’s vector similarity search to find facts semantically related to the topic. A query like "employment" will match "Paul works at Google" even though the word “employment” doesn’t appear in the fact.

print("=== Topic: 'work and employment' ===")
for fact in store.search("work and employment"):
    print(f"  {fact}")

print()
print("=== Topic: 'family relationships' ===")
for fact in store.search("family relationships"):
    print(f"  {fact}")

print()
print("=== Topic: 'hobbies and leisure' ===")
for fact in store.search("hobbies and leisure"):
    print(f"  {fact}")

title: Memory
text:
D - Controlling Search Results
Two optional parameters let you narrow what search() returns:

n_results: caps the number of results (default 10). Results are always ordered by relevance (lowest distance first).
max_distance: cosine-distance threshold (0 = identical, 2 = maximally dissimilar). Facts with a distance above this value are excluded. Useful values are roughly 0.3 (tight match) to 0.6 (broad match). Defaults to None (no cutoff).


# Cap the number of results
top_facts = store.search("people and where they live or work", n_results=2)
print("Top 2 facts (n_results=2):")
for fact in top_facts:
    print(f"  {fact}")

print()

# Filter by cosine distance; only return closely matching facts
print("Tight filter (max_distance=0.4):")
tight = store.search("work and employment", max_distance=0.4)
for fact in tight:
    print(f"  {fact}")

print()

# Looser filter (broader recall)
print("Loose filter (max_distance=0.7):")
loose = store.search("work and employment", max_distance=0.7)
for fact in loose:
    print(f"  {fact}")

title: Memory
text:
E - Deleting Facts
delete() removes all stored facts that exactly match the given string.

print(f"Before deletion: {len(store)} facts")

store.delete("Emma lives in Paris")

print(f"After deletion:  {len(store)} facts")

# Confirm it's gone
results = store.search("Paris")
print(f"\nSearch for 'Paris' after deletion: {results}")

title: Memory
text:
F - Listing All Facts
list_all() returns every stored fact string, unordered.

all_facts = store.list_all()
print(f"All {len(all_facts)} stored facts:")
for fact in sorted(all_facts):
    print(f"  {fact}")

title: Memory
text:
G - Persistent Storage
Pass a persist_path to save facts to disk. The store survives process restarts and can be shared across sessions.

import tempfile
import shutil

# Use a temp directory so this notebook is self-contained
persist_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="memory_store_")

# Session 1: populate
store1 = SemanticMemoryStore(persist_path=persist_dir)
store1.store("Paul works at Google")
store1.store("Sarah is the sister of Emma")
print(f"Session 1: stored {len(store1)} facts to {persist_dir}")

# Session 2: reload from disk (simulating a new process)
store2 = SemanticMemoryStore(persist_path=persist_dir)
print(f"Session 2: loaded {len(store2)} facts from disk")
for fact in store2.search("people"):
    print(f"  {fact}")

# Clean up
shutil.rmtree(persist_dir)

title: Memory
section: H - Using SemanticMemoryStore with a Model Client
text:
H - Using SemanticMemoryStore with a Model Client
A natural integration pattern: retrieve relevant facts from the memory store and inject them into the model’s system prompt before a conversation, giving the model grounded knowledge about the user or domain.

# Retrieve facts relevant to an upcoming conversation topic
topic = "Paul's professional background"
relevant_facts = store.search(topic, n_results=3)

# Build a system prompt grounded in stored facts
facts_block = "\n".join(f"- {f}" for f in relevant_facts)
system_prompt = f"""You are a helpful assistant. Here are some facts you know about the user:

{facts_block}

Use these facts to personalise your responses."""

print("Generated system prompt:")
print(system_prompt)

# To use with a model client:
# from aimu.models import OllamaClient as ModelClient
# model_client = ModelClient(ModelClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
# model_client.system_message = system_prompt
# response = model_client.chat("What do you know about my job?")

title: Memory
text:
I - DocumentStore: Basic Setup
DocumentStore stores content at named paths (e.g. /preferences.md, /notes/meeting.md). It mirrors Anthropic’s Managed Agents Memory API, making it a drop-in replacement in local deployments. By default the store is ephemeral; pass persist_path to save documents to disk.

from aimu.memory import DocumentStore

# In-memory store (ephemeral)
docs = DocumentStore()

print(f"Document store created. Documents stored: {len(docs)}")

title: Memory
text:
J - Writing and Reading Documents
write(path, content) creates or overwrites a document at the given path. read(path) retrieves it. Paths are arbitrary strings; use a directory-like convention for organisation.

docs.write("/preferences.md", "Always use concise responses.\nPrefer bullet points over prose.")
docs.write("/notes/meeting-2024-01.md", "Discussed Q3 roadmap with team.\nDecided to prioritise API improvements.")
docs.write("/notes/meeting-2024-02.md", "Reviewed roadmap progress. API work on track.")
docs.write("/profile.md", "Name: Paul\nRole: Senior Engineer\nTeam: Platform")

print(f"Documents stored: {len(docs)}")
print()

# Read a document back
content = docs.read("/preferences.md")
print("=== /preferences.md ===")
print(content)

title: Memory
text:
K - Editing Documents
edit(path, old_str, new_str) replaces the first occurrence of old_str with new_str in the named document. Raises ValueError if old_str is not found.

print("Before edit:")
print(docs.read("/preferences.md"))

docs.edit("/preferences.md", "concise responses", "detailed, thorough responses")

print("\nAfter edit:")
print(docs.read("/preferences.md"))

title: Memory
text:
L - Listing and Searching Documents
list_paths(prefix) returns sorted paths, optionally filtered by a path prefix. search_full_text(query) does a case-insensitive substring search across all documents and returns a list of {"path": ..., "content": ...} dicts.

# List all paths
print("All paths:")
for path in docs.list_paths():
    print(f"  {path}")

print()

# List only paths under /notes/
print("Paths under /notes/:")
for path in docs.list_paths(prefix="/notes/"):
    print(f"  {path}")

print()

# Full-text search
print("Full-text search for 'roadmap':")
for result in docs.search_full_text("roadmap"):
    print(f"  {result['path']}: {result['content'][:60]}...")

title: Memory
text:
M - Deleting Documents
delete(path) removes the document at the given path. It is a no-op if the path doesn’t exist.

print(f"Before deletion: {len(docs)} documents")
print(f"Paths: {docs.list_paths()}")

docs.delete("/notes/meeting-2024-01.md")

print(f"\nAfter deletion: {len(docs)} documents")
print(f"Paths: {docs.list_paths()}")

title: Memory
text:
N - DocumentStore Persistent Storage
Pass persist_path to save documents to disk. Documents are stored as individual files and reloaded automatically on the next instantiation.

import tempfile
import shutil

persist_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="doc_store_")

# Session 1: write documents
docs1 = DocumentStore(persist_path=persist_dir)
docs1.write("/preferences.md", "Always use concise responses.")
docs1.write("/profile.md", "Name: Paul\nRole: Senior Engineer")
print(f"Session 1: stored {len(docs1)} documents to {persist_dir}")

# Session 2: reload from disk
docs2 = DocumentStore(persist_path=persist_dir)
print(f"Session 2: loaded {len(docs2)} documents from disk")
for path in docs2.list_paths():
    print(f"  {path}: {docs2.read(path)[:40]}...")

# Clean up
shutil.rmtree(persist_dir)

title: Memory
text:
O - Memory as Agent Tools
make_memory_tools(store) wraps any MemoryStore instance as three @aimu.tool-decorated functions (store_memory, search_memories, and list_memories) that an agent can call directly. This is the in-process alternative to the FastMCP servers in aimu.memory.mcp / aimu.memory.document_mcp.
Unlike the image, audio, and speech built-in tools, there is no lazy singleton here: the store instance is always explicit because persistence semantics (ephemeral vs. on-disk, which persist_path, semantic vs. document) are meaningful choices the caller should make deliberately.

from aimu.memory import SemanticMemoryStore
from aimu.tools.builtin import make_memory_tools

store = SemanticMemoryStore()
memory_tools = make_memory_tools(store)

# Three @tool-decorated functions, ready to pass to Agent(tools=...)
for t in memory_tools:
    spec = t.__tool_spec__["function"]
    params = list(spec["parameters"]["properties"].keys())
    print(f"{spec['name']}({', '.join(params)}):  {spec['description'].splitlines()[0]}")


from aimu.models import ModelClient, OllamaModel
from aimu.agents import Agent

store = SemanticMemoryStore()
agent = Agent(
    ModelClient(OllamaModel.QWEN_3_5_9B),
    system_message="You are a helpful assistant with access to a persistent memory store. "
    "Use store_memory to save important facts the user shares, "
    "and search_memories before answering questions about the user.",
    tools=make_memory_tools(store),
)

# Store a fact
print(agent.run("Please remember that my preferred programming language is Python."))
print()

# Retrieve it in a fresh turn
print(agent.run("What do you know about my programming preferences?"))

title: Prebuilt Agents
text:
Demonstrates the orchestrator + worker tools pattern using three ready-to-use prebuilt agents from aimu.agents.prebuilt.
Each agent class wraps an orchestrator Agent that coordinates specialist worker sub-agents exposed as @aimu.tool-decorated dispatch functions. The LLM decides which workers to call, in what order, and how many times.
All three agents are subclasses of OrchestratorAgent (from aimu.agents), which provides the run() and messages interface and handles the tool wiring. The last section shows how to subclass OrchestratorAgent to build your own orchestrator agent.

title: Prebuilt Agents
text:
ResearchReportAgent
An orchestrator Agent autonomously coordinates three worker sub-agents. The LLM decides which workers to call, in what order, and how many times, rather than following a predetermined code path.
research-report-agent  (orchestrator)
  ├─ [tool] research_overview   →  overview-worker      (Agent)
  ├─ [tool] find_examples       →  examples-worker      (Agent)
  └─ [tool] find_counterpoints  →  counterpoints-worker (Agent)

Setup
Create an OllamaClient and pass it to ResearchReportAgent. The agent internally creates fresh client instances for each worker.

from aimu.models import OllamaClient
from aimu.agents.prebuilt import ResearchReportAgent

model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
agent = ResearchReportAgent(model_client)

print("Orchestrator model:", model_client.model.value)
print("Worker tools:", [t.__name__ for t in model_client.tools])



Live Web Search (optional)
ResearchReportAgent accepts an optional worker_tools= list of @aimu.tool-decorated functions. When provided, each worker gets access to those tools (e.g. web search, page fetching) and the orchestrator automatically enables concurrent worker dispatch: all three workers can be called in a single round-trip when the model returns multiple tool calls at once.
# Requires SEARXNG_BASE_URL env var pointing to a SearXNG instance
from aimu.tools import builtin

agent = ResearchReportAgent(model_client, worker_tools=[builtin.web_search, builtin.get_webpage])
# Workers can now call: web_search(query, num_results), get_webpage(url).
# The orchestrator will dispatch all three workers concurrently.
Without worker_tools=, workers are pure text-only agents (the default shown below).


Run
Call agent.run() with a research topic. The orchestrator will call each worker tool, then synthesize the results into a final report.

task = "What is retrieval-augmented generation?"

result = agent.run(task)
print(result)

Inspect the orchestrator’s message history to see the full tool-calling sequence.

agent.messages



Streaming
run(stream=True) yields StreamChunk objects. TOOL_CALLING chunks show which worker was invoked and the worker’s response; GENERATING chunks are the orchestrator’s synthesis.

from aimu.models import StreamingContentType

task = "What is retrieval-augmented generation?"

for chunk in agent.run(task, stream=True):
    if chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.TOOL_CALLING:
        worker = chunk.content["name"]
        response_preview = chunk.content["response"][:80].replace("\n", " ")
        print(f"\n[tool: {worker}]\n  {response_preview}...\n")
    elif chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.GENERATING:
        print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)


del agent, model_client

title: Prebuilt Agents
text:
CodeReviewAgent
An orchestrator Agent autonomously coordinates three specialist reviewer sub-agents. The LLM decides which reviewers to engage based on the submitted code.
code-review-agent  (orchestrator)
  ├─ [tool] review_security     →  security-reviewer    (Agent)
  ├─ [tool] review_performance  →  performance-reviewer (Agent)
  └─ [tool] review_readability  →  readability-reviewer (Agent)

Setup
Create an OllamaClient and pass it to CodeReviewAgent.

from aimu.models import OllamaClient
from aimu.agents.prebuilt import CodeReviewAgent

model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
agent = CodeReviewAgent(model_client)

print("Orchestrator model:", model_client.model.value)
print("Reviewer tools:", [t.__name__ for t in model_client.tools])



Sample Code
A short snippet with an SQL injection vulnerability and an O(n²) search loop: two issues that span different review dimensions.

code_snippet = """
import sqlite3

def get_user(db_path, username):
    conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    # Fetch user record
    cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + username + "'")
    return cursor.fetchone()

def find_duplicates(items):
    dupes = []
    for i in range(len(items)):
        for j in range(i + 1, len(items)):
            if items[i] == items[j] and items[i] not in dupes:
                dupes.append(items[i])
    return dupes
"""



Run
Pass the code to agent.run(). The orchestrator will call each specialist reviewer, then synthesize findings into a prioritized report.

result = agent.run(f"Review this Python code:\n\n{code_snippet}")
print(result)

Inspect the orchestrator’s message history to see each reviewer call and its response.

agent.messages



Streaming
TOOL_CALLING chunks reveal which reviewer was called and a preview of their findings; GENERATING chunks are the orchestrator’s synthesis.

from aimu.models import StreamingContentType

for chunk in agent.run(f"Review this Python code:\n\n{code_snippet}", stream=True):
    if chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.TOOL_CALLING:
        reviewer = chunk.content["name"]
        response_preview = chunk.content["response"][:80].replace("\n", " ")
        print(f"\n[tool: {reviewer}]\n  {response_preview}...\n")
    elif chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.GENERATING:
        print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)


del agent, model_client

title: Prebuilt Agents
text:
ContentCreationAgent
An orchestrator Agent builds content step by step. The LLM determines how many sections to write and how many times to call each worker.
content-creation-agent  (orchestrator)
  ├─ [tool] research_topic   →  research-worker  (Agent)
  ├─ [tool] create_outline   →  outline-worker   (Agent)
  └─ [tool] write_section    →  section-writer   (Agent)

Setup
Create an OllamaClient and pass it to ContentCreationAgent.

from aimu.models import OllamaClient
from aimu.agents.prebuilt import ContentCreationAgent

model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
agent = ContentCreationAgent(model_client)

print("Orchestrator model:", model_client.model.value)
print("Worker tools:", [t.__name__ for t in model_client.tools])



Run
Give the agent a content brief. It will research the topic, build an outline, write each section, then assemble the final piece.

task = "Write about the benefits of test-driven development as a blog post for software developers"

result = agent.run(task)
print(result)

Inspect the orchestrator’s message history to trace the research → outline → write loop.

agent.messages



Streaming
TOOL_CALLING chunks show which worker was called (and reveal the step-by-step construction); GENERATING chunks are the orchestrator assembling the final output.

from aimu.models import StreamingContentType

task = "Write about the benefits of test-driven development as a blog post for software developers"

for chunk in agent.run(task, stream=True):
    if chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.TOOL_CALLING:
        worker = chunk.content["name"]
        response_preview = chunk.content["response"][:80].replace("\n", " ")
        print(f"\n[tool: {worker}]\n  {response_preview}...\n")
    elif chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.GENERATING:
        print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

title: Prebuilt Agents
text:
Build Your Own Orchestrator Agent
All three example agents follow the same pattern because they inherit from OrchestratorAgent. There are two ways to build your own:

OrchestratorAgent.assemble(client, system_message, workers=[...]): no subclass needed. Each worker Agent is auto-wrapped as a @aimu.tool named after the worker, with the description taken from the worker’s system_message. Use this when each worker can be dispatched by passing the user’s task verbatim.
Subclass OrchestratorAgent for full control over each dispatch tool (custom names, descriptions, signatures). The pattern:

Create worker Agent instances (one per specialization)
Wrap each worker’s .run() in an @aimu.tool-decorated dispatch function
Call self._init_orchestrator(model_client, name=..., system_message=..., tools=[...]), and that’s it


run() and messages are inherited from OrchestratorAgent. The next cell shows the subclass form.

from aimu.agents import Agent, OrchestratorAgent
from aimu.models import OllamaClient
from aimu.models.model_client import ModelClient
import aimu


class TranslationAgent(OrchestratorAgent):
    def __init__(self, model_client):
        translator = Agent(
            ModelClient(model_client.model),
            "Translate the given text accurately. Output only the translation.",
            name="translator",
        )
        proofreader = Agent(
            ModelClient(model_client.model),
            "Review the translation for errors and return the corrected version.",
            name="proofreader",
        )

        @aimu.tool
        def translate(text_and_target_language: str) -> str:
            """Translate text to the specified target language."""
            return translator.run(text_and_target_language)

        @aimu.tool
        def proofread(translated_text: str) -> str:
            """Review and correct a translation for accuracy and naturalness."""
            return proofreader.run(translated_text)

        self._init_orchestrator(
            model_client,
            name="translation-agent",
            system_message=(
                "You are a translation coordinator. "
                "First use the translate tool to translate the text, "
                "then use the proofread tool to check the result. "
                "Return the final proofread translation."
            ),
            tools=[translate, proofread],
        )


model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
agent = TranslationAgent(model_client)

result = agent.run("Translate to French: 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog'")
print(result)

title: Prebuilt Agents
text:
Clean Up

del agent, model_client

title: Agent Skills
text:
Demonstrate AIMU’s skill support: giving agents reusable, on-demand capabilities via SKILL.md files.
Skills are discovered from the filesystem, injected as a catalog into the agent’s system message, and loaded on demand via an activate_skill MCP tool call. Python scripts inside a skill’s scripts/ directory are automatically exposed as additional MCP tools.
What this notebook covers: - Discovering and inspecting skills with SkillManager - The skills MCP server (build_skills_server) - Running a SkillAgent that discovers and activates skills - Streaming skill-aware agent output - Script tools from a skill’s scripts/ directory - SkillAgent.from_config with skill_dirs

title: Agent Skills
text:
Setup
Skills are directories containing a SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter followed by markdown instructions. Two sample skills ship with AIMU under examples/skills/, exposed as aimu.paths.skills (== aimu.paths.examples / "skills").

from aimu import paths

skills_dir = paths.examples / "skills"  # aimu.paths.skills points here too
conv_dir = skills_dir / "unit-converter"

print("Skills directory:", skills_dir)
for d in sorted(skills_dir.iterdir()):
    print(f"  {d.name}/SKILL.md")

title: Agent Skills
text:
A - Discovering Skills with SkillManager
SkillManager scans one or more directories for skill subdirectories, each containing a SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter. By default it scans .agents/skills/ and .claude/skills/ at both project and user scope. Here we pass skill_dirs to point it at our sample directory.

from aimu.skills import SkillManager

manager = SkillManager(skill_dirs=[str(skills_dir)])

print(f"Discovered {len(manager.skills)} skill(s):")
for name, skill in manager.skills.items():
    print(f"  {name!r:25s}  {skill.description[:70]}")

catalog_prompt() builds the XML block that gets injected into the agent’s system message. This is the catalog tier (tier 1) of progressive disclosure: only names and descriptions, no full instructions.

print(manager.catalog_prompt())

get_skill_body() loads the full markdown instructions for a skill; this is what the model receives when it calls activate_skill (tier 2 of progressive disclosure).

print(manager.get_skill_body("haiku-poet"))

title: Agent Skills
text:
B - The Skills MCP Server
build_skills_server() creates an in-process FastMCP server from a SkillManager. It registers an activate_skill tool and one tool per Python script found in any skill’s scripts/ directory. Wrap it in MCPClient(server=...) to use it directly.

from aimu.skills import build_skills_server
from aimu.tools import MCPClient

skills_server = build_skills_server(manager)
skills_client = MCPClient(server=skills_server)

print("Tools exposed by the skills server:")
for tool in skills_client.list_tools():
    print(f"  {tool.name}")

You can call activate_skill directly through the client to inspect what the model receives:

result = skills_client.call_tool("activate_skill", {"name": "unit-converter"})
print(result.content[0].text)

title: Agent Skills
text:
C - SkillAgent
SkillAgent extends Agent with skill support. Pass a SkillManager at construction (or omit it to auto-discover from default paths). On the first run(), _setup_skills() fires once: it appends the skill catalog to the system message and attaches the skills MCPClient. The model then decides which skills are relevant and calls activate_skill to load them.
You can inspect what gets injected into the system message before running:

from aimu.models import OllamaClient
from aimu.agents import SkillAgent

model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
model_client.system_message = "You are a helpful assistant. Always activate the most relevant skill before answering."

agent = SkillAgent(
    model_client,
    name="skilled-assistant",
    max_iterations=6,
    skill_manager=SkillManager(skill_dirs=[str(skills_dir)]),
)

# Peek at the system message after skill injection (before run)
agent._setup_skills()
print("--- System message (truncated) ---")
print(model_client.system_message[:500], "...")


# Fresh client; run() calls _setup_skills() automatically on first invocation
model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
model_client.system_message = "You are a helpful assistant. Always activate the most relevant skill before answering."

agent = SkillAgent(
    model_client,
    name="skilled-assistant",
    max_iterations=6,
    skill_manager=SkillManager(skill_dirs=[str(skills_dir)]),
)

result = agent.run("Write me a haiku about autumn rain.")
print(result)


# Inspect the full message history to see the activate_skill tool call
for msg in model_client.messages:
    role = msg["role"]
    if role == "system":
        print("[system]  (omitted; contains catalog)")
    elif role == "user":
        print(f"[user]    {msg['content']}")
    elif role == "assistant" and "tool_calls" in msg:
        for tc in msg["tool_calls"]:
            print(f"[tool→]  {tc['function']['name']}({tc['function']['arguments']})")
    elif role == "tool":
        preview = str(msg["content"])[:120].replace("\n", " ")
        print(f"[←tool]  {msg['name']}: {preview}...")
    elif role == "assistant":
        print(f"[asst]    {str(msg['content'])[:200]}")

title: Agent Skills
text:
D - Streaming SkillAgent Output
run(stream=True) yields StreamChunk objects. The TOOL_CALLING phase shows when the model activates a skill, and GENERATING shows the final answer produced using the loaded instructions.

from aimu.models import StreamingContentType

model_client2 = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)
model_client2.system_message = "You are a helpful assistant. Always activate the most relevant skill before answering."

agent2 = SkillAgent(
    model_client2,
    name="streamer",
    max_iterations=6,
    skill_manager=SkillManager(skill_dirs=[str(skills_dir)]),
)

current_iteration = -1
current_phase = None

for chunk in agent2.run("How many kilometres is 26.2 miles (a marathon)?", stream=True):
    if chunk.iteration != current_iteration:
        current_iteration = chunk.iteration
        print(f"\n--- iteration {current_iteration} ---")

    if chunk.phase != current_phase:
        current_phase = chunk.phase
        print(f"\n[{chunk.phase.value}]")

    if chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.THINKING:
        print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)
    elif chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.TOOL_CALLING:
        print(f"  {chunk.content['name']}({chunk.content['arguments']!r}) → {str(chunk.content['response'])[:120]}")
    elif chunk.phase == StreamingContentType.GENERATING:
        print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

title: Agent Skills
text:
E - Script Tools
A skill can include Python scripts in a scripts/ subdirectory. Each *.py file is automatically registered as an MCP tool named {skill_name}__{script_stem}. Scripts are run via subprocess and their stdout is returned as the tool result.
Here we add a scripts/now.py script to the unit-converter skill:

# Add a scripts/ directory to the unit-converter skill
scripts_dir_path = conv_dir / "scripts"
scripts_dir_path.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

(scripts_dir_path / "now.py").write_text("import datetime\nprint(datetime.datetime.now().isoformat())\n")

print("Script added:", scripts_dir_path / "now.py")
print("Contents:")
print((scripts_dir_path / "now.py").read_text())


# Rebuild the skills server; it now picks up the new script tool
manager3 = SkillManager(skill_dirs=[str(skills_dir)])  # re-discover to pick up scripts/
server3 = build_skills_server(manager3)
client3 = MCPClient(server=server3)

print("Tools exposed (including script tools):")
for tool in client3.list_tools():
    print(f"  {tool.name}")
    if tool.description:
        print(f"      {tool.description}")


# Call the script tool directly
result3 = client3.call_tool("unit-converter__now", {})
print("unit-converter__now returned:", result3.content[0].text)

title: Agent Skills
text:
F - SkillAgent.from_config
SkillAgent.from_config accepts a skill_dirs key to create a SkillManager automatically. Omit skill_dirs to auto-discover skills from the standard search paths (.agents/skills/ and .claude/skills/ at project and user scope).

model_client4 = OllamaClient(OllamaClient.MODELS.QWEN_3_5_9B)

agent4 = SkillAgent.from_config(
    {
        "name": "cfg-agent",
        "system_message": "You are a helpful assistant. Activate relevant skills before answering.",
        "max_iterations": 6,
        "skill_dirs": [str(skills_dir)],
    },
    model_client4,
)

print("Agent name:      ", agent4.name)
print("Skill manager:   ", agent4.skill_manager)
print("Skills found:    ", list(agent4.skill_manager.skills.keys()))


result4 = agent4.run("Write a haiku about the ocean at dawn.")
print(result4)

title: Agent Skills
text:
G - Clean Up

# Remove the scripts/ directory added in section E
import shutil

shutil.rmtree(conv_dir / "scripts", ignore_errors=True)

del model_client, model_client2, model_client4, agent, agent2, agent4
print("Cleaned up.")

title: Tools
text:
AIMU supports two routes for giving an agent tools, and both end up as callables in one place: the agent’s tools list:
Because both are just callables in the agent’s tools, you mix them with + and there is a single dispatch path (on a name collision the last entry wins). Executing tools is the agent’s job: the model client only advertises tools and records the model’s tool-call requests. This notebook covers both.

title: Tools
text:
A - @aimu.tool decorator
Decorate a Python function with @aimu.tool. The decorator attaches an OpenAI-format spec at __tool_spec__ and leaves the function otherwise unchanged.

import aimu


@aimu.tool
def letter_counter(word: str, letter: str) -> int:
    """Count occurrences of a letter in a word."""
    return word.lower().count(letter.lower())


letter_counter.__tool_spec__

Hand decorated functions to an Agent via the tools= kwarg. The agent advertises them to the model each turn and dispatches the calls in-process, without an MCP round-trip.

from aimu.models import ModelClient, OllamaModel
from aimu.agents import Agent

client = ModelClient(OllamaModel.QWEN_3_5_9B)
agent = Agent(client, tools=[letter_counter], max_iterations=3)

print(agent.run("How many r's are in the word strawberry?"))


Argument validation
Before a tool runs, the arguments the model supplied are validated and lax-coerced against the function’s type hints. A stringified scalar like "5" becomes 5 for an int parameter; a value that can’t be coerced, a missing required argument, or an unknown argument raises ToolArgumentError. At dispatch the agent catches that and hands the message back to the model as a tool result, so the model can correct itself and retry.
aimu.tools.coerce_tool_arguments(fn, arguments) is the same function the dispatch path calls; here we invoke it directly to see both outcomes.

from aimu.tools import ToolArgumentError, coerce_tool_arguments


@aimu.tool
def set_volume(level: int) -> str:
    """Set the volume (0-100)."""
    return f"volume={level}"


# A coercible mismatch is coerced to the declared type.
print(coerce_tool_arguments(set_volume, {"level": "40"}))  # -> {'level': 40}

# An uncoercible value is rejected; the tool body never runs.
try:
    coerce_tool_arguments(set_volume, {"level": "loud"})
except ToolArgumentError as exc:
    print(exc)



Per-run tool override
Agent(tools=...) sets the agent’s configured tools. To use a different set for a single run, without mutating that state, pass tools= to Agent.run(). tools=None (default) uses the configured tools; any other value, including [] to disable tools, applies to that run only and is restored afterward. Since MCP tools are also just callables in the agent’s tools (via as_tools()), the override covers them too.

from aimu.tools import builtin

# Configure the agent with two tools...
agent = Agent(client, tools=[letter_counter, builtin.calculate], max_iterations=3)

# ...but expose only `letter_counter` for this single run.
print(agent.run("How many e's in 'effervescence'?", tools=[letter_counter]))

# Configured tools are still in place afterward.
print("configured tools:", [fn.__name__ for fn in agent.tools])



Tool context (dependency injection)
A tool often needs shared state (a store, a cache, configuration) that the model should neither see nor supply. Declare a ctx: ToolContext[Deps] parameter and AIMU fills it at call time from the agent’s deps= field (or a per-run run(deps=) override), while hiding it from the tool’s JSON schema. This replaces module-level globals and keeps tools unit-testable.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

from aimu import ToolContext


@dataclass
class Notes:
    items: dict = field(default_factory=dict)


@aimu.tool
def save_note(ctx: ToolContext[Notes], key: str, text: str) -> str:
    """Save a note under a key."""
    ctx.deps.items[key] = text
    return f"saved {key}"


# `ctx` is injected by the agent, so the model-facing schema lists only key + text:
print("schema params:", list(save_note.__tool_spec__["function"]["parameters"]["properties"]))

notes = Notes()
agent = Agent(client, tools=[save_note], deps=notes, max_iterations=3)
agent.run("Save a note under 'todo' that says 'water the plants'.")
print("stored:", notes.items)

title: Tools
text:
B - Built-in tools
aimu.tools.builtin exposes a small library of general-purpose tools, each decorated with @aimu.tool and ready to drop into any agent: echo, get_current_date_and_time, get_weather, calculate, get_webpage, search, list_directory, read_file. builtin.ALL_TOOLS is the full list.
These same callables are also registered on the aimu/tools/mcp.py FastMCP server so they can be reused cross-process. See section D below.

from aimu.tools import builtin

# Use a few selected tools
agent = Agent(client, tools=[builtin.calculate, builtin.get_current_date_and_time], max_iterations=4)
print(agent.run("What's 17 * 23 plus the current minute?"))

title: Tools
text:
C - MCPClient with file server
For cross-process tools, point MCPClient at a Python file that implements an MCP server. AIMU ships aimu/tools/mcp.py which exposes the same built-in tools as section B.

from aimu.tools import MCPClient
from aimu import paths

mcp_client = MCPClient(file=str(paths.package / "tools" / "mcp.py"))
mcp_client.list_tools()

Once connected, you can list all available tools provided by the MCP server. This shows the tools, their descriptions, and required parameters.

mcp_client.call_tool("echo", {"echo_string": "Hello, world!"})

You can call individual tools by their name and pass the required parameters. The tool will execute and return its result.

title: Tools
text:
D - MCPClient with configuration
For multiple MCP servers, or a server that must be launched as a subprocess, provide a configuration dictionary.

config = {
    "mcpServers": {"aimu": {"command": "python", "args": ["-m", "aimu.tools.mcp"]}},
}

mcp_client = MCPClient(config=config)
mcp_client.list_tools()

The configuration-based client provides the same interface for listing and calling tools, but can aggregate tools from multiple servers.

mcp_client.call_tool("echo", {"echo_string": "Hello, world!"})

Tools work exactly the same way regardless of how the MCPClient was initialized.

title: Tools
section: E - MCPClient with in-memory FastMCP server
text:
E - MCPClient with in-memory FastMCP server
For ad-hoc tools defined inline, pass a FastMCP instance directly. No subprocess; tools live in your Python process but go through the MCP protocol.

from fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("AIMU Tools")


@mcp.tool()
def echo(echo_string: str) -> str:
    return echo_string


mcp_client = MCPClient(server=mcp)
mcp_client.list_tools()

This approach creates an MCP server with tools defined using Python decorators, then wraps it with an MCPClient for easy access.

mcp_client.call_tool("echo", {"echo_string": "Hello, world!"})

The FastMCP-based client works identically to file-based and configuration-based clients, providing a consistent interface for tool interaction.

Attaching MCP tools to an agent: as_tools()
call_tool() above is for using a server standalone. To let a model use the server’s tools, call mcp_client.as_tools(). It returns one @aimu.tool-style callable per server tool (each carries its __tool_spec__ and dispatches cross-process behind the scenes). Pass them to Agent(tools=...), mixing freely with in-process @aimu.tool functions.
Keep a reference to the MCPClient (the callables hold one, so the connection stays alive). The list is a snapshot; call as_tools() again to pick up server-side changes.

# mcp_client wraps the in-memory `echo` server from section E.
mcp_tools = mcp_client.as_tools()
print("MCP tools as callables:", [fn.__name__ for fn in mcp_tools])

# Pass them to an agent, mixed with an in-process @tool. Last-in-list wins on
# a name collision, so a local override would go after mcp_tools.
agent = Agent(client, tools=mcp_tools + [letter_counter])
print("agent tools:", [fn.__name__ for fn in agent.tools])

title: Tools
section: F - Web search and retrieval tools
text:
F - Web search and retrieval tools
The built-in search and get_webpage tools demonstrate live external lookups. search queries a SearXNG instance (set SEARXNG_BASE_URL to point at your own); get_webpage fetches a URL and strips HTML to visible text.

mcp_client = MCPClient(file=str(paths.package / "tools" / "mcp.py"))
mcp_client.list_tools()


mcp_client.call_tool("search", {"query": "Python programming"})


mcp_client.call_tool("get_webpage", {"url": "https://www.python.org/"})

title: Vision Input
text: AIMU’s ModelClient.chat() accepts an optional images= argument for vision-capable models. Image inputs are normalized to OpenAI-style content blocks internally, then adapted per-provider where needed (Anthropic, Ollama-native, HuggingFace).

title: Vision Input
text:
What this notebook covers

The supports_vision capability flag on Model enums and the VISION_MODELS classproperty
Passing images as file paths, raw bytes, data: URLs, and http(s):// URLs
Multiple images in one turn
Vision against OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and Ollama (native)
The capability check that raises ValueError on non-vision models

title: Vision Input
text:
Setup
API-key-based providers read OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, and GOOGLE_API_KEY from your environment (or a .env file in the project root). Ollama needs a vision-capable model pulled locally (e.g. ollama pull gemma3:12b).

import base64
import io
from pathlib import Path

from PIL import Image

# Build a tiny test image we can use with any vision model.
img = Image.new("RGB", (64, 64), color=(255, 128, 0))
buf = io.BytesIO()
img.save(buf, format="PNG")
image_bytes = buf.getvalue()

image_path = Path("./test_image.png")
image_path.write_bytes(image_bytes)

image_data_url = "data:image/png;base64," + base64.b64encode(image_bytes).decode("ascii")
print("file:", image_path, "bytes:", len(image_bytes))

title: Vision Input
text:
1. Capability flags
Every Model enum member carries a supports_vision boolean. Each provider client also exposes a VISION_MODELS classproperty mirroring THINKING_MODELS and TOOL_MODELS.

from aimu.models import OpenAIClient, AnthropicClient, GeminiClient, OllamaClient

for client_cls in (OpenAIClient, AnthropicClient, GeminiClient, OllamaClient):
    print(client_cls.__name__, "vision models:", [m.name for m in client_cls.VISION_MODELS])

Create a ModelClient for the first set of examples.

from aimu.models import ModelClient
from aimu.models.ollama import OllamaModel

client = ModelClient(OllamaModel.GEMMA_4_E4B)

title: Vision Input
text:
2. Single image: file path
The simplest case: pass a file path string. AIMU reads the file, base64-encodes it, and inlines it as a data:image/... URL.

response = client.chat("What color is this image?", images=[str(image_path)])
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
3. Single image: raw bytes
Useful when the image is already in memory (e.g. a Streamlit upload, a screenshot, or output from PIL). Bytes are encoded as image/png by default.

response = client.chat("Describe what you see in two short sentences.", images=[image_bytes])
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
4. Single image: data URL (already encoded)
If you have a data:image/...;base64,... string already, pass it through unchanged.

response = client.chat("Is this image warm or cool toned?", images=[image_data_url])
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
5. Multiple images in one turn
Pass a list. The model sees them in order, alongside the text prompt.

img2 = Image.new("RGB", (64, 64), color=(0, 128, 255))
buf2 = io.BytesIO()
img2.save(buf2, format="PNG")
image_bytes_2 = buf2.getvalue()

response = client.chat(
    "Compare these two images and tell me how their colors differ.",
    images=[image_bytes, image_bytes_2],
)
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
6. Anthropic Claude
AIMU converts the OpenAI-format content blocks to Anthropic’s native image blocks (base64 source) at request time; nothing in your code changes.

from aimu.models.anthropic.anthropic_client import AnthropicModel

client = ModelClient(AnthropicModel.CLAUDE_HAIKU_4_5)
response = client.chat("What is the dominant color in this image?", images=[image_bytes])
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
7. Google Gemini
Gemini uses Google’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so image_url blocks pass through directly.

from aimu.models.openai_compat.gemini_client import GeminiModel

client = ModelClient(GeminiModel.GEMINI_2_5_FLASH)
response = client.chat("What color is this image?", images=[image_bytes])
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
8. Ollama (native API)
AIMU rewrites image_url content blocks to Ollama’s message-level images= field at request time. Requires a vision-capable model pulled locally (Gemma 3, Gemma 4, etc.).

from aimu.models.ollama.ollama_client import OllamaModel

client = ModelClient(OllamaModel.GEMMA_3_12B)  # pull first: ollama pull gemma3:12b
response = client.chat("What color is this image?", images=[image_bytes])
print(response)

title: Vision Input
text:
9. Capability check
Passing images= to a non-vision model raises ValueError up front, before any API call.

client = ModelClient(OllamaModel.QWEN_3_8B)
try:
    client.chat("Describe this.", images=[image_bytes])
except ValueError as e:
    print("caught:", e)

title: Vision Input
text:
10. Stateless one-shot: generate(images=)
chat(images=...) keeps the turn in client.messages for follow-ups. For a one-shot “look once and answer” call (captioning, scoring, classification) that should not accumulate history, use generate(images=...), the stateless sibling of chat(). Each call stands alone; no reset() needed between calls.

vision_client = ModelClient(OllamaModel.GEMMA_4_E4B)

caption = vision_client.generate("Caption this image in one short sentence.", images=[str(image_path)])
print(caption)

# generate() never touches message state; the client stays stateless across calls.
print("messages after generate():", vision_client.messages)

title: Vision Input
text:
Cleanup

image_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)

title: Conversations
text:
ConversationManager is AIMU’s persistent chat history layer. It stores OpenAI-format messages (role/content) in a TinyDB JSON file, attaches a timestamp each time you write, and supports multiple independent conversations in the same database. Messages returned via the messages property are copies, so callers can mutate them freely without corrupting persisted state.
Use it when a chat needs to survive process restarts: a Streamlit/Gradio app that resumes the user’s last session, a CLI tool that keeps a running log, or any place where ModelClient.messages (which is in-memory only) isn’t enough. The integration pattern is simple: drive your model client’s chat() calls as usual, then push model_client.messages to the manager after each turn. Pass use_last_conversation=True on startup to pick up where the previous session left off.

title: Conversations
text:
What this notebook covers







Section
Topic




A
Creating a ConversationManager backed by a TinyDB JSON file


B

C

D

title: Conversations
text:
A - Basic Conversation Manager Setup
The ConversationManager class provides persistent storage for chat conversations using TinyDB. It automatically handles conversation creation, message storage, and timestamping.

from aimu.history import ConversationManager
import tempfile
import os

# Create a temporary database file for this example
temp_db = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".json", delete=False)
temp_db.close()

# Initialize a new conversation manager
conv_manager = ConversationManager(db_path=temp_db.name)

print(f"Created conversation manager with database: {temp_db.name}")
print(f"Initial messages: {len(conv_manager.messages)}")

title: Conversations
text:
B - Adding Messages to Conversation
Messages are stored as dictionaries with role and content. The ConversationManager automatically adds timestamps when updating conversations.

# Create initial messages for a conversation
initial_messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Hello, can you help me with Python programming?"},
    {
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": "Of course! I'd be happy to help you with Python programming. What specific topic would you like to explore?",
    },
]

# Update the conversation with these messages
conv_manager.update_conversation(initial_messages)

print(f"Added {len(initial_messages)} messages to the conversation")
print(f"Total messages: {len(conv_manager.messages)}")
print("\nCurrent messages:")
for i, msg in enumerate(conv_manager.messages):
    print(f"  {i + 1}. {msg['role']}: {msg['content'][:50]}...")
    print(f"     Timestamp: {msg.get('timestamp', 'N/A')}")

title: Conversations
text:
C - Continuing a Conversation
You can continue adding messages to an existing conversation. The manager tracks which messages are new and adds timestamps accordingly.

import time

# Get current messages and add more
current_messages = conv_manager.messages
new_messages = current_messages + [
    {"role": "user", "content": "I want to learn about list comprehensions."},
    {
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": "List comprehensions are a concise way to create lists in Python. Here's a basic example: [x**2 for x in range(5)] creates [0, 1, 4, 9, 16].",
    },
]

# Small delay to show timestamp difference
time.sleep(0.1)

# Update with the extended conversation
conv_manager.update_conversation(new_messages)

print(f"Extended conversation to {len(conv_manager.messages)} messages")
print("\nAll messages with timestamps:")
for i, msg in enumerate(conv_manager.messages):
    print(f"  {i + 1}. [{msg.get('timestamp', 'N/A')}] {msg['role']}: {msg['content'][:60]}...")

title: Conversations
text:
D - Working with Multiple Conversations
You can create multiple conversations by creating new ConversationManager instances or by explicitly starting new conversations.

# Start a new conversation in the same manager
new_doc_id, new_messages = conv_manager.create_new_conversation()
print(f"Created new conversation with ID: {new_doc_id}")
print(f"New conversation messages: {len(new_messages)}")

# Note: The current manager is still using the original conversation
print(f"Current manager still has: {len(conv_manager.messages)} messages")

# Create a second manager for a different topic
temp_db2 = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".json", delete=False)
temp_db2.close()

conv_manager2 = ConversationManager(db_path=temp_db2.name)

# Add different content to the second conversation
math_messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Can you help me with calculus?"},
    {
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": "Absolutely! Calculus deals with rates of change and areas under curves. What specific topic in calculus would you like to explore?",
    },
]

conv_manager2.update_conversation(math_messages)

print(f"\nSecond conversation has {len(conv_manager2.messages)} messages")
print("Second conversation content:")
for msg in conv_manager2.messages:
    print(f"  {msg['role']}: {msg['content'][:50]}...")

title: Conversations
text:
E - Resuming Previous Conversations
You can resume the last conversation by setting use_last_conversation=True when initializing the ConversationManager.

# Close the first manager
conv_manager.close()

# Create a new manager that resumes the last conversation
resumed_manager = ConversationManager(db_path=temp_db.name, use_last_conversation=True)

print(f"Resumed conversation with {len(resumed_manager.messages)} messages")
print("\nResumed conversation content:")
for i, msg in enumerate(resumed_manager.messages):
    print(f"  {i + 1}. {msg['role']}: {msg['content'][:60]}...")

# Continue the resumed conversation
continued_messages = resumed_manager.messages + [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Can you show me more advanced list comprehension examples?"},
    {
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": "Sure! Here are some advanced examples:\n- Nested: [[x*y for x in range(3)] for y in range(2)]\n- Conditional: [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]",
    },
]

resumed_manager.update_conversation(continued_messages)
print(f"\nContinued conversation now has {len(resumed_manager.messages)} messages")

title: Conversations
text:
F - Message Properties and Structure
Messages returned by the ConversationManager are copies, so you can safely manipulate them without affecting the stored conversation.

# Get messages (returns a copy)
messages_copy = resumed_manager.messages

print(f"Retrieved {len(messages_copy)} messages")
print("\nMessage structure analysis:")

for i, msg in enumerate(messages_copy[:3]):  # Show first 3 messages
    print(f"\nMessage {i + 1}:")
    print(f"  Keys: {list(msg.keys())}")
    print(f"  Role: {msg['role']}")
    print(f"  Content length: {len(msg['content'])} characters")
    print(f"  Has timestamp: {'timestamp' in msg}")
    if "timestamp" in msg:
        print(f"  Timestamp: {msg['timestamp']}")

# Modify the copy - this won't affect the stored conversation
messages_copy[0]["content"] = "Modified content"
print("\nModified copy, but original remains unchanged:")
print(f"Copy first message: {messages_copy[0]['content'][:20]}...")
print(f"Original first message: {resumed_manager.messages[0]['content'][:20]}...")

title: Conversations
text:
G - Integration with Model Clients
The ConversationManager can be integrated with model clients to automatically persist chat history.

# Simulate a model client integration pattern
class SimpleModelClient:
    def __init__(self, conversation_manager):
        self.conv_manager = conversation_manager

    def chat(self, user_message):
        # Simulate model response
        responses = {
            "hello": "Hello! How can I help you today?",
            "python": "Python is a versatile programming language. What would you like to know?",
            "goodbye": "Goodbye! Feel free to ask if you have more questions.",
        }

        # Simple keyword-based response
        assistant_response = "I understand. Can you tell me more?"
        for keyword, response in responses.items():
            if keyword.lower() in user_message.lower():
                assistant_response = response
                break

        # Update conversation with both messages
        current_messages = self.conv_manager.messages
        new_messages = current_messages + [
            {"role": "user", "content": user_message},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_response},
        ]

        self.conv_manager.update_conversation(new_messages)
        return assistant_response


# Create a client with our conversation manager
client = SimpleModelClient(resumed_manager)

# Have a conversation
print("Starting conversation with integrated client:")
print(f"Initial messages: {len(client.conv_manager.messages)}")

response1 = client.chat("Hello there!")
print("\nUser: Hello there!")
print(f"Assistant: {response1}")

response2 = client.chat("Tell me about Python")
print("\nUser: Tell me about Python")
print(f"Assistant: {response2}")

print(f"\nFinal message count: {len(client.conv_manager.messages)}")

title: Conversations
text:
H - Cleanup and Resource Management
The ConversationManager automatically closes the database connection when deleted, but you can also explicitly close it.

# Show final state of conversations
print("Final conversation states:")
print(f"Main conversation: {len(resumed_manager.messages)} messages")
print(f"Second conversation: {len(conv_manager2.messages)} messages")

# Explicitly close the managers
resumed_manager.close()
conv_manager2.close()

print("\nClosed all conversation managers")

# Clean up temporary files
try:
    os.unlink(temp_db.name)
    os.unlink(temp_db2.name)
    print("Cleaned up temporary database files")
except OSError:
    print("Note: Some temporary files may need manual cleanup")

title: AIMU Notebooks
text:
Runnable tutorial notebooks for AIMU, ordered to build understanding incrementally. They are authored as plain-text Quarto .qmd files (markdown with executable python cells), so they diff cleanly and are easy to edit or hand to an AI assistant.
Most notebooks need a live backend (a running Ollama server, a cloud API key, or a GPU for HuggingFace image/audio models) and gracefully skip sections when a dependency is missing. By default they are not executed at render time (see _quarto.yml); run a cell yourself in Jupyter, or quarto preview a notebook, to execute against your own backend.

title: Model Client
text:
ModelClient is AIMU’s unified entry point for talking to any supported backend: local (Ollama, HuggingFace, llama-cpp-python, any OpenAI-compatible server) or cloud (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini). The same call shape (generate(), chat(), chat(..., stream=True)) works against every provider, so you can swap models without rewriting the surrounding code. Streaming yields a single StreamChunk type with explicit THINKING, TOOL_CALLING, and GENERATING phases. The client advertises tools and records the model’s tool-call requests, but executing tools in a loop is the job of an Agent (notebook 07); image input is built into the base client.
Use a ModelClient whenever you want a turn-by-turn conversation with a model: building a chat UI, prototyping prompts, wiring up tools, or experimenting with thinking/reasoning models. For autonomous tool-calling loops on top of the same client, see notebook 07 (Agents).

title: Model Client
text:
What this notebook covers







Section
Topic




A
Stateless text generation with generate(), streaming, and generation kwargs


B
Multi-turn chat, message history, and custom system messages


C
Thinking models: accessing last_thinking and filtering streamed phases


D
Tool calling: wrapping the client in an Agent with an in-memory MCP server


E
Parallel tool calls in one model turn


F
Releasing resources

title: Model Client
text:
A - Text Generation
ModelClient is a factory that accepts any provider model enum and automatically selects the right backend. The cell below picks the model used throughout this notebook; change model_enum to try a different provider or model.

from aimu.models import ModelClient, HAS_OLLAMA, HAS_HF

# Pick the model used throughout this notebook. Swap this assignment to try a
# different backend; every later cell reads `model_enum` unchanged.
if HAS_OLLAMA:
    from aimu.models import OllamaModel

    model_enum = OllamaModel.QWEN_3_5_9B
elif HAS_HF:
    from aimu.models import HuggingFaceModel

    model_enum = HuggingFaceModel.QWEN_3_8B
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Install a provider backend, e.g. `pip install aimu[ollama]`.")

The ModelClient factory dispatches to the right backend based on the model enum type. You can also instantiate a specific client directly when you prefer explicit control:
from aimu.models import ModelClient, OllamaModel

model_client = ModelClient(OllamaModel.QWEN_3_5_9B)  # factory: picks OllamaClient automatically

# Or directly:
from aimu.models import OllamaClient
model_client = OllamaClient(OllamaModel.QWEN_3_5_9B)
Here, we’ll use the model enum assigned above.

model_client = ModelClient(model_enum)
print(model_client.model.name + "\n" + model_client.model.value)

For non-interactive (chat-based) generation, the generate method is used. This method allows you to specify a prompt and receive a generated response.

model_client.generate("Name the capital of France.")

If the model supports thinking/reasoning, the thinking output for generation can be accessed via model_client.last_thinking.

model_client.last_thinking

Output can be streamed as well.

response = model_client.generate("Name the capital of England.", stream=True)

for chunk in response:
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

The thinking output can be filtered by specifying which streaming content types to include.

from aimu.models import StreamingContentType

response = model_client.generate(
    "Name the capital of the United States.", stream=True, include=[StreamingContentType.GENERATING]
)

for chunk in response:
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

Parameters can be passed to the generate (and chat) methods to control the output. For thinking/reasoning models, max_tokens should be large enough to capture both the reasoning output, which happens first, and the generation output.
For generate_kwargs, see https://github.com/ollama/ollama/blob/main/docs/modelfile.md#valid-parameters-and-values

model_client.generate(
    "Name the capital of Germany.",
    generate_kwargs={
        "temperature": 1.0,
        "top_p": 0.9,
        "top_k": 40,
        "max_tokens": 1024,
        "repeat_penalty": 1.1,
    },
)

title: Model Client
text:
B - Basic Chat
Chatting with a model simply involves passing a message and receiving a response. The Model Client handles the conversation history automatically.

model_client.chat("What is the capital of China?")

Chat responses can be streamed as well.

for chunk in model_client.chat("How long has it been the capital?", stream=True):
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

The complete message history, including the model thinking/reasoning, can be accessed via the messages property of the Model Client.

model_client.messages

A custom system message can be set, or re-set, as desired.

model_client.system_message = "You are a helpful assistant that responds using pirate speak."
model_client.messages[0]


model_client.chat("Name a famous pirate from there.")

title: Model Client
text:
C - Model Thinking
Some models support extended reasoning (also called “thinking”), where the model works through a problem step-by-step before producing a final response. AIMU automatically enables thinking for models in THINKING_MODELS (e.g., DEEPSEEK_R1_8B, QWEN_3_5_9B, GPT_OSS_20B).
